upcoming_elections.race: OC Board of Supervisors District 4 — Open Seat (Chaffee termed out)
candidates: Fred Jung (Fullerton Mayor, $354K cash on hand — dominant fundraiser)
Connor Traut (Buena Park Vice Mayor, $225K, OC Dem Party + Chaffee endorsement)
upcoming_elections.relevance: CRITICAL: Four-way primary, top-two advance to November. District is 40.4% D, 30.5% R. Determines whether Democrats keep 3-2 board majority. Jung's $354K and Traut's $225K + party endorsement make them likely top-two. Filing closed March 6. Next finance filing April 23.
upcoming_elections.action: Research all four candidates on housing, transit, homelessness, and budget positions. Jung and Traut are the fundraising leaders. Consider donations and volunteering for preferred candidate.

upcoming_elections.race: OC Board of Supervisors District 5 — Foley (D) vs Dixon (R)
candidates: Katrina Foley (D, incumbent, $285K cash, Board Vice Chair)
Diane Dixon (R, Assemblywoman, $135K cash, outraised Foley in H2 2025)
upcoming_elections.relevance: CRITICAL: Most vulnerable Democratic seat. District is most Republican of the 5 (38.1% R vs 32.8% D). Foley won 51-49 in 2022. Harris won D5 by just 318 votes (49-48%). If Dixon wins, board flips regardless of District 4 outcome. Arguably the most consequential OC race.
upcoming_elections.action: Research both candidates. Support Foley if aligned with your priorities. Dixon outraised Foley in H2 2025 — financial momentum matters. This is the defensive counterpart to District 4's offensive opportunity.

candidates: Avelino Valencia (D, current AD-68 Assemblymember — frontrunner)
Josh Newman (D, former State Senator SD-29, served 2016-2018 and 2020-2024)
Rhonda Shader (R, former Placentia mayor, 2022 runner-up)
upcoming_elections.relevance: Doug's direct state senator race. Filing closed March 6. Three candidates: two Democrats and one Republican. Top-two primary likely produces D-D general election given district lean. Both Dems have legislative experience.
upcoming_elections.action: Compare Valencia's Assembly record vs. Newman's Senate record on housing mandates, RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy. With two Dems, evaluate which is stronger on housing enforcement.

upcoming_elections.race: State Assembly AD-68 — Open Seat (Valencia running for Senate)
candidates: Jessie Lopez (D, Santa Ana city councilor)
David Peñaloza (D, Santa Ana city councilor — endorsed by Valencia)
upcoming_elections.relevance: Doug's state assembly district. Open because Valencia is running for Senate. All-Democratic field means primary effectively decides the seat. Valencia endorsed Peñaloza.
upcoming_elections.action: Compare both candidates' Santa Ana council voting records on housing and development. Valencia's endorsement of Peñaloza signals continuity. Evaluate both on housing mandate enforcement.

upcoming_elections.race: City of Orange — Mayor + 3 Council Seats
upcoming_elections.relevance: Direct local representation. Housing Element compliance is the top issue. Who sits on this council determines whether Orange builds housing or evades state mandates.
upcoming_elections.action: Track candidate announcements. Evaluate on housing and development positions.

upcoming_elections.race: City of Irvine — Mayor + 3 Council Seats
upcoming_elections.relevance: Neighboring city. General Plan update with 57,656 units proposed. Irvine Company monopoly is the backdrop.
upcoming_elections.action: Monitor for impact on regional housing supply.

upcoming_elections.race: California Governor — Open Seat (Newsom termed out)
upcoming_elections.relevance: HIGH STAKES: Housing is #1 voter issue. Governor controls housing mandate enforcement (RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals). Major risk: Dem vote fragmentation in top-two primary could produce R-R November runoff — no Dem candidate won party endorsement at February convention. Porter and Becerra strongest on state enforcement against local obstruction.
upcoming_elections.action: Research candidates' housing enforcement positions. Vote strategically in top-two primary to avoid Dem fragmentation. The next governor determines whether the OC housing fight has a state ally or not.

upcoming_elections.relevance: DA and Sheriff shape public safety approach countywide.

key_political_dynamics:
Orange County is one of America's few truly purple counties. The area around UCI Medical Center sits at the exact fulcrum — the City of Orange is split 36.6% D to 34.8% R. Elections here are decided by razor margins (the 2024 CD-45 race was decided by 581 votes). California's state housing mandates (SB 9, RHNA, Builder's Remedy) create intense friction with local governments that resist density. The 2026 elections are unusually high-stakes: the Board of Supervisors majority, multiple open state legislative seats, city council races in both Orange and Irvine, and new congressional maps from Prop 50 all converge. For a moderate Democrat, this is the most target-rich political environment in America.

March 2026 updates: (1) BoS District 4 fundraising revealed: Jung $354K, Traut $225K with Dem party endorsement — clear top-two favorites. District is 40.4% D. (2) BoS District 5: Dixon outraised Foley by $50K in H2 2025. Harris won D5 by just 318 votes — the tightest margin in OC. (3) City of Orange achieved Housing Element compliance (not on Newsom's March 25 noncompliant list), but 696 Builder's Remedy units still in play. (4) Irvine Oak Creek proposal evolved: 50-acre nature park added, $90.4M community benefit fees, but ballot measure controversy over 1988 open space initiative. Council approved MOU 7-0. (5) OC Streetcar 95% complete, fares set at $2/$5 day pass, service 6am-11pm daily. (6) SD-34 is a three-way race: Valencia (D), Newman (D), Shader (R). AD-68 set: Lopez (D) vs Peñaloza (D, Valencia-endorsed). (7) Governor's race: 8+ Dems, 2-3 Rs. Housing is #1 issue. Risk of Dem vote fragmentation producing R-R November.

Key difference from Austin #1: Purple county in a blue state (vs blue city in red state). OC voter registration: 36.4% D, 33.9% R, 23.3% NPP. Doug's vote carries MORE weight in local races than in Austin. Elections here are decided by hundreds of votes, not thousands.

Key difference #2: California's state framework is an ally, not an enemy. In Texas, the state legislature actively preempts Austin's progressive policies. In California, the state government MANDATES progressive housing policy (SB 9, SB 10, RHNA, Builder's Remedy) that local governments resist. The YIMBY fight flips: instead of pushing local government to be more progressive, you're pushing local government to comply with state mandates it's trying to evade. Newsom's March 25 final warning to 15 noncompliant cities — with AG referral threatened — reinforces the enforcement posture.

Key difference #3: The Irvine Company monopoly. Irvine Company (Donald Bren, America's richest real estate developer) controls ~75% of Irvine's apartment units and much of the commercial real estate. Their proposed 3,100-unit Oak Creek Golf Course conversion is the first major implementation test of the General Plan. This is a unique housing dynamic — one private entity has near-monopoly power over rents and development in a city of 314,000 people.

immediate.detail: registertovote.ca.gov — update registration with new OC address as soon as you have one
immediate.reason: Must be registered to vote in June 2026 primary (registration deadline ~May 18)
immediate.llm_prompt: I'm moving from Austin, TX to Orange, CA (92868) and need to register to vote in California. Walk me through the process at registertovote.ca.gov. What are the key deadlines for the June 2, 2026 primary? Can I register online or do I need to do it in person? What ID do I need? Also explain California's top-two primary system and how it differs from Texas primaries.

immediate.reason: This is the Austin Monitor equivalent for OC — the single most important local news source

Help me understand what information I need to look up once I have my specific address: city council district, county supervisor district, school district, water district, and any other relevant jurisdictions. What websites do I use to look these up in Orange County? Also explain the difference between the City of Orange and Orange County governance structures.

this_month.action: Research OC Board of Supervisors District 4 candidates
this_month.detail: This is the highest-stakes local race — determines whether Dems keep 3-2 board majority. June 2026 primary.
this_month.reason: Board majority defense is the single most impactful race in OC
this_month.llm_prompt: I need to research the candidates for the Orange County Board of Supervisors District 4 open seat race in the June 2, 2026 primary. This seat determines whether Democrats maintain their 3-2 board majority. Help me: (1) find who has declared or is expected to run, (2) understand what the Board of Supervisors controls (budget, land use, public health, etc.), (3) create a comparison framework focused on housing, transit, and fiscal policy, (4) identify where to find candidate information in OC.

this_month.action: Read City of Orange Housing Element status
this_month.detail: State rejected their plan in 2023. Builder's Remedy applications pending. Understand where the city stands.
this_month.reason: Housing Element compliance is the core YIMBY fight in OC — enforcement not advocacy
this_month.llm_prompt: Explain the City of Orange, CA Housing Element situation. The state (HCD) rejected their housing plan in 2023. What does this mean practically? What is the Builder's Remedy and how does it apply? What projects are pending under Builder's Remedy in Orange? How does this connect to RHNA (Regional Housing Needs Assessment) compliance? I'm a YIMBY moving to Orange and want to understand the housing policy landscape.

medium_term.action: Attend one People for Housing OC event
medium_term.detail: Only 2 staffers — they need volunteers. This is the highest-alignment org in OC.

medium_term.action: Attend one City of Orange council meeting
medium_term.detail: Get a feel for the council dynamics, public comment process, and housing issues on the agenda.
medium_term.reason: Direct observation of your city government

strategic.detail: Connect with People for Housing OC, Kennedy Commission, California YIMBY, Irvine Community Land Trust. Understand who's doing what and where the gaps are.
strategic.reason: OC's YIMBY infrastructure is thinner than Austin's — there may be high-leverage gaps to fill
strategic.llm_prompt: I'm a YIMBY moving from Austin, TX to Orange County, CA. In Austin, the YIMBY ecosystem is well-developed (AURA, Austin YIMBY Action, etc.). OC seems thinner — People for Housing OC has only 2 staffers. Help me map the OC housing advocacy ecosystem: who are the key organizations, what are their focuses, where are the gaps? How does California YIMBY (state-level) interact with local OC groups? What role does the Kennedy Commission play? Is there a need for new organizing, or should I plug into existing groups?

strategic.detail: Maintain Austin connections (District 1 race, AURA) while building OC engagement. Target: 1 hour/week per location.
strategic.reason: The dual-location model only works if both locations get consistent attention
